items.previewText:
A $20k check can cost more time than a $2M check.

If it comes from the wrong investor. Paul Graham’s warning:
**inexperienced investors can be the most dangerous**.

They’re nervous.
They move slowly.
They over-lawyer.

And the work can be wildly disproportionate to the dollars. Example from YC:
A

Want to sound smart? Talk more.
Want to be right? Talk less—and qualify everything. Paul Graham’s hero **Robert Morris** had an unusual trait:

“He’s never wrong.”

It sounds impossible.
But Paul argues it’s not about omniscience.
It’s about restraint. The method is simple (and rare):

**Don’t say a

The real “new economy” isn’t internet stocks.

It’s this: **good ideas win faster**. Graham’s meta-theme across bubble-era trends:

- good ideas count more
- young founders with vision gain edge over old connections
- doing good work matters more than dressing up
- people get rewarded closer to the

Writing to persuade and writing to discover are **diametrically opposed**.

The more your conclusion disagrees with what people already believe,
the more energy you’ll waste selling it instead of having it.

Randomness doesn’t create free will.

It destroys the “ghost in the machine” just as effectively as determinism.
